summary: Drew DeVault recaps his first FOSDEM experience in 2019, describing a whirlwind of socializing, volunteering, and community building. He met Arch Linux developers to discuss modernizing their package maintenance, coordinated with Wayland ecosystem developers on standardization, and held meetups for both sr.ht and sway that drew nearly 50 attendees. The post highlights the strength of the 'hallway track' — informal conversations and connections — as the most valuable part of the conference. He also notes receiving a Librem 5 dev board from Purism for wlroots development.
key_points: The hallway track and informal socializing proved more valuable than formal sessions, enabling coordination across projects like Wayland, sway, and sr.ht
DeVault discussed helping Arch Linux migrate away from Subversion and was invited to become a Trusted User
The sr.ht and sway meetups drew unexpectedly large turnout with surprisingly little overlap between the two communities
Discussions about unifying and standardizing the Wayland ecosystem took place with KDE and other collaborators
Purism gifted a Librem 5 dev board to the wlroots team for improving touch support
money_quotes: My scatterbrained interests throughout the domain of free software came back to haunt me as I struggled to keep up with all of the people I had to meet & thank, all of the sessions I wanted to see, and all of the dinners & outings I wanted to attend.
The hallway track continued to be the highlight of the event.
The turnout for both rooms was way more than I expected - almost 50! It seemed like every seat was filled.
A particularly interesting tangent had me defending the email choice to a skeptic and getting a lot of good feedback and insights from the rest of the room.
We started off with a bang by releasing sway 1.0-rc1.

Together we also explained our somewhat novel philosophy on project governance that I credit the success of the project to. It's designed to maximize contributors, and it's entirely to their credit that the success of sway and wlroots is owed.
key_insight: The most productive outcomes at open source conferences happen through informal hallway conversations and community meetups rather than formal presentations.
